serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Methi Malai Mutter is approximately one bowl of 300 g, providing around 450 kcal, and is typically accompanied by roti, naan, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Methi Malai Mutter are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using component-level data for fenugreek leaves, green peas, cream, and cashew nuts. No single composite entry for this dish exists in the ICMR-NIN tables; values represent a weighted recipe estimate consistent with standard North Indian restaurant preparation. Core macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients.

gi_source.title: Anti-diabetic effects of Trigonella foenum-graecum (fenugreek): A review
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Methi Malai Mutter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 42 is an analogous estimate grounded in: (1) peer-reviewed evidence from the Journal of Ethnopharmacology documenting the hypoglycemic and GI-lowering properties of fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum); (2) the well-established low GI of green peas (~48, University of Sydney GI Database); and (3) the fat-mediated carbohydrate absorption delay from cream and cashew paste. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (42 × 13.33 × 3.0) / 100 ≈ 16.8 / 2 ≈ 7.9 GL units for a 300 g serving (39.99 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 250 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 750 mg sodium, representing ~33% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt during preparation; the potassium content (195 mg/100 g) from peas partially offsets sodium load, but cream-heavy versions should be consumed sparingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~42) driven by fenugreek's soluble fibre and hypoglycemic compounds, fat-mediated carbohydrate absorption delay, and moderate carbohydrate density (13.33 g/100 g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Methi Malai Mutter is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (150–200 g) without added sugar; fenugreek's galactomannan fibre slows glucose absorption, and pairing with a low-GI bread such as whole-wheat roti further moderates postprandial glycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 4.0 g/100 g from cream and cashews; a 300 g serving provides ~12 g saturated fat, approaching the 13 g daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 150 g and consider substituting heavy cream with low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat intake; the dish's potassium, magnesium, and fenugreek-derived anti-inflammatory compounds are cardioprotective when saturated fat is moderated.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fenugreek leaves contain anti-inflammatory saponins and flavonoids; however, saturated fat from cream may be mildly pro-inflammatory at high intake.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Methi Malai Mutter is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to fenugreek's anti-inflammatory properties; use reduced cream or substitute with low-fat alternatives to minimise pro-inflammatory saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Methi Malai Mutter is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry originating from the Punjab region of India. The dish combines three principal ingredients: fresh fenugreek leaves (methi), tender green peas (mutter or matar), and fresh cream (malai). The gravy is built by sautéing onions with ginger and garlic, then blending in cashew nut paste and fresh cream to create a silky, ivory-white sauce. Aromatic whole spices — green cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves — are tempered in oil or ghee at the start to infuse the base with warmth. The fenugreek leaves contribute a characteristic pleasant bitterness that is balanced by the natural sweetness of the peas and the richness of the cream. The texture is smooth and velvety with soft pea pieces throughout. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 3.33 g protein, 9.33 g fat (4.0 g saturated), and 13.33 g carbohydrate with 2.33 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 250 mg/100 g. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 42, attributed to fenugreek's soluble galactomannan fibre and the fat-mediated slowing of carbohydrate absorption. Fenugreek is clinically documented for its hypoglycemic properties, making this dish particularly relevant for blood sugar management. It is suitable for vegetarians, halal diets, and gluten-free diets. Methi Malai Mutter is typically served at lunch or dinner alongside naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice, and is a popular choice at North Indian restaurants and during festive or celebratory meals.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component-level data for fenugreek leaves, green peas, cream, and cashew nuts; no single composite entry for Methi Malai Mutter exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate supported by peer-reviewed fenugreek literature and the known GI of green peas. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite North Indian curry preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this prepared dish is not reported in the available approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g of 0.17 g/100 g is retained from the input record; this value is plausible given the use of dairy cream (naturally occurring trans-vaccenic acid) and is consistent with USDA dairy fat composition data. 4. Glycemic load per serving (300 g) calculated as (42 × 39.99) / 100 ≈ 16.8, rounded to 7.9 per 150 g half-serving reference; full 300 g serving GL ≈ 16.8. 5. Duplicate portion entries in the input (four identical '1 serving 200g' entries) were consolidated; canonical serving size set to 300 g bowl consistent with restaurant norms. 6. The input listed 'grabfood' as a source for cholesterol, saturated_fat, and trans_fat; grabfood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2. These values have been retained only where they are consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA component data and are now attributed to ICMR-NIN/USDA. 7. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
